stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to ED 5 days (120 hours) after non-consensual unprotected vaginal intercourse with an unknown male at a party. She did not present sooner because of fear of judgement. She is otherwise well, has no medical history, no allergies, takes no regular medications, has a regular 28-day menstrual cycle with last period 11 days ago (so currently around day 11 and within the fertile window), and has previously had her three-dose HPV vaccine and a full primary hepatitis B course as a child. She has had no further intercourse since. She is uncertain about reporting to police but wants medical care. The local sexual assault medical service is contactable, the on-call gynaecology registrar can insert a copper IUD today, and the assailant's HIV status is unknown. She is asking what can still be offered for emergency contraception and infection prevention given the 5-day window since exposure, and she is open to all options including a long-acting method She declines police involvement today and asks specifically about emergency contraception that will still work at the 120-hour mark and about whether STI screening can be done at the same visit.
lead_in: Which of the following is the most appropriate component of management at this time?

options.text: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg orally as a single-dose emergency contraceptive within the 72-hour licensed window
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel EC efficacy is limited to within 96 hours of unprotected intercourse and is not first-line beyond that window (Family Planning Australia; eTG).

options.text: Ulipristal acetate 30 mg orally as a single-dose emergency contraceptive within the 120-hour licensed window
options.rationale: Ulipristal extends the window to 120 hours but evidence is for up to 120 hours post-intercourse, and at exactly 120 hours efficacy is borderline; the copper IUD is the more reliable option still available now (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Copper IUD insertion as emergency contraception, with empirical STI prophylaxis and sexual assault referral
options.rationale: Copper IUD inserted up to 5 days after the earliest predicted ovulation is the most effective emergency contraception (failure rate <1%) and is the only option licensed beyond 120 hours. At day 11 of a 28-day cycle she remains within the fertile window. Empirical STI prophylaxis (ceftriaxone, azithromycin or doxycycline, metronidazole) and warm referral to the sexual assault service complete management (RANZCOG; ASHM Post-Exposure Prophylaxis Guidelines).

options.text: Commence HIV non-occupational post-exposure prophylaxis with tenofovir-emtricitabine-dolutegravir for a full 28-day course
options.rationale: HIV PEP must be commenced within 72 hours of exposure; at 120 hours the window has closed and PEP is not indicated (ASHM PEP Guidelines).

options.text: Accelerated hepatitis B vaccination course because the patient is presumed unprotected after childhood priming
options.rationale: She has a documented completed primary hepatitis B course; check anti-HBs titre rather than re-vaccinate (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

stem: A 29-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed at 5 weeks postpartum at her general practice. She has focal epilepsy controlled on carbamazepine 400 mg BD (level stable, no seizures for 3 years), is exclusively breastfeeding her baby (infant weight tracking the 50th centile, no concerns at the maternal-child health nurse review), and had a normal vaginal birth with no thromboembolic history, no migraine, normotensive, BMI 24, non-smoker, and a normal Hb of 122 g/L. She has read about long-acting reversible contraception and is aware that carbamazepine is an enzyme inducer. She wishes to space the next pregnancy by at least 3 years and prefers a method that does not require daily adherence or rely on her remembering to attend for 3-monthly injections. She is happy with all options being on the table, including LARCs and combined methods, and asks the GP to recommend the most reliable option that will not be undermined by her antiepileptic. Her neurologist is aware and supportive of any contraceptive choice She has no history of menorrhagia and no copper allergy, and lactational amenorrhoea alone is not acceptable to her given the 3-year spacing she wants.
lead_in: Which contraceptive option is the most appropriate first-line recommendation?

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant (Implanon NXT) inserted in the inner upper arm
options.rationale: Enzyme-inducing AEDs (including carbamazepine) significantly reduce etonogestrel plasma levels via CYP3A4 induction; the implant is NOT recommended (UKMEC 3) in women on enzyme inducers because contraceptive efficacy is reduced (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyloestradiol 30 microg with levonorgestrel) taken daily
options.rationale: Enzyme inducers reduce COCP efficacy and the COCP is generally avoided <6 weeks postpartum if breastfeeding due to milk supply and VTE concerns; not first-line here (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (norethisterone 350 microg) taken at the same time every day
options.rationale: POP efficacy is reduced by enzyme inducers; not recommended (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine device (Mirena) or copper IUD inserted as a LARC
options.rationale: Intrauterine devices (LNG-IUD and copper IUD) deliver hormone locally or are non-hormonal and have efficacy that is NOT reduced by enzyme inducers; both are UKMEC 1 in women on enzyme-inducing AEDs and compatible with breastfeeding. They provide the long-acting reversible spacing this woman requests (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Drug Interactions with Hormonal Contraception).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg intramuscularly every 12 weeks as her contraceptive method
options.rationale: DMPA contraceptive efficacy is not significantly reduced by enzyme inducers (UKMEC 1), so it is acceptable; however it is not the SINGLE BEST recommendation when an IUD is suitable and the woman wants long-term spacing without quarterly injections, and DMPA has bone density and weight effects to discuss (Family Planning Australia; FSRH UKMEC).

stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman is reviewed 6 weeks postpartum at her general practice. She had a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass 3 years ago with weight loss from 142 kg to 78 kg (current BMI 28, stable). She is bottle feeding by choice, normotensive, non-smoker, no migraine, no VTE history, and is on regular vitamin and iron supplementation post-bariatric. She is asking the GP for contraception and is happy with all options, including LARCs. Her last pregnancy was carefully monitored post-bariatric and was uncomplicated other than mild iron deficiency. She specifically asks whether she can simply take her sister's combined oral contraceptive pill, because she has heard that any oral hormonal method is reliable. She also asks whether a return of regular cycles is required before she starts a method, given she is not yet menstruating because she is using progesterone-containing analgesia Her dietician has confirmed she is taking her bariatric multivitamin daily and her ferritin has normalised, and the GP is happy to insert a LARC at this visit if appropriate She specifically asks whether oral pills will still work post-bypass.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate consideration when selecting contraception for this woman?

options.text: Oral hormonal contraception is unaffected because Roux-en-Y bypass does not alter small-molecule absorption
options.rationale: Roux-en-Y bypass alters oral hormonal absorption and reduces COCP and POP efficacy; FSRH advises against reliance on oral methods after malabsorptive bariatric procedures (FSRH UKMEC; FSRH Overweight/Obesity and Contraception).

options.text: Long-acting reversible methods are preferred because oral hormonal absorption is reduced post-bypass
options.rationale: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass bypasses the proximal small bowel and reduces absorption of oral hormonal contraceptives, which lowers their reliability. LARCs (LNG-IUD, copper IUD, etonogestrel implant, DMPA) do not depend on enteric absorption and are first-line after bariatric bypass surgery (Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare; UKMEC; RANZCOG).

options.text: Combined hormonal contraception is preferred to ensure cycle regularity post-bariatric surgery in this patient
options.rationale: Oral COCP efficacy is reduced after RYGB; transdermal/vaginal CHC may also be considered but post-bariatric weight loss instability and weight rebound risk make LARC the preferred first-line, not CHC for cycle control (FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

options.text: Await return of regular menstrual cycles before starting any contraception in this patient
options.rationale: Fertility returns rapidly postpartum (and dramatically improves post-bariatric); contraception should be in place to avoid early conception (Family Planning Australia; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

options.text: Permanent sterilisation should be recommended because pregnancy is unsafe after bariatric surgery
options.rationale: Pregnancy after bariatric is safe with planned spacing (12-24 months recommended) and adequate nutritional surveillance; recommending sterilisation is not appropriate without patient-led discussion (RANZCOG; FSRH Overweight/Obesity).

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman undergoes elective surgical TOP at 13 weeks by dilation and evacuation in a day-procedure unit. The procedure itself is uneventful but in recovery 30 minutes later she is found to be soaking the pad with bright red blood. She has no significant past medical history, is on no anticoagulation, has no known bleeding diathesis, BMI 26, and no prior caesarean section. On urgent review: BP 88/52, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 99%, T 36.7, alert but pale and clammy. Abdominal palpation reveals a soft, poorly contracted, palpable uterus midway between the umbilicus and the symphysis pubis. Speculum: brisk red bleeding from a parous cervical os, no obvious cervical laceration, no visible retained products at the os. Estimated blood loss is already 800 mL. The major haemorrhage protocol is being activated, two large-bore IV cannulae are in situ, and a group-and-hold has been sent. The most likely cause is uterine atony given the soft, poorly contracted uterus The on-call gynaecologist is en route; pharmacy has misoprostol, ergometrine and carboprost available The anaesthetic team has secured the airway adequately for now.
lead_in: What is the single best initial uterotonic intervention while ongoing resuscitation continues?

options.text: IV oxytocin 5 IU slow bolus followed by oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1 L saline
options.rationale: Postabortal uterine atony is managed identically to postpartum atony: oxytocin is the first-line uterotonic, given as a slow 5 IU IV bolus followed by an infusion. Second-line agents (ergot alkaloids, carboprost, misoprostol) and tranexamic acid are added if atony persists; mechanical and surgical measures escalate from there (RANZCOG PPH; WHO PPH).

options.text: Misoprostol 800 microg per rectum as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Misoprostol is a third-line uterotonic in Australian PPH/TOP haemorrhage protocols, used when first-line agents are unavailable or contraindicated; IV oxytocin is the first-line (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV as the sole haemorrhage agent, withholding any uterotonic for now
options.rationale: TXA is a useful adjunct (1 g IV within 3 h of the onset of haemorrhage) but does not address atony; uterotonics are the primary mechanism-targeted therapy (RANZCOG PPH; WOMAN trial).

options.text: Carboprost 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Carboprost is second-line after oxytocin and ergometrine; it is contraindicated in asthma. It is not the first-line agent (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Methylergometrine 250 microg intramuscularly as the first-line uterotonic, given before any IV oxytocin
options.rationale: Ergometrine/methylergometrine is the standard second-line after oxytocin and is contraindicated in hypertension and pre-eclampsia. First-line uterotonic is oxytocin (RANZCOG PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

stem: A 64-year-old woman presents with 4 hours of intermittent retrosternal chest tightness, worse on exertion, partially relieved by rest. She has type 2 diabetes on metformin, hypertension on perindopril, and is a current smoker. She has had two similar episodes in the past week. On arrival she is pain-free. Her ECG is in sinus rhythm with non-specific T-wave flattening in the lateral leads, no ST elevation or depression, no Q-waves, and is unchanged from a tracing 6 months ago. Initial high-sensitivity cardiac troponin I (hs-cTnI) drawn 4 hours after pain onset is 14 ng/L (99th percentile cut-off 16 ng/L; algorithm low-rule-out less than 5, rule-in greater than 52). A 1-hour repeat is 18 ng/L (absolute change 4 ng/L; algorithm rule-in delta greater than 6). Her renal function is normal, and she has no prior cardiac history or stress test. The cardiology registrar asks whether the patient sits in the rule-out, rule-in or observe zone and what the 3-hour repeat threshold is for this assay. The cardiology consultant on call has been notified and is en route. The patient is currently pain-free, eating, drinking and comfortable in resus, with a normal mental state, no signs of heart failure and stable vital signs while she awaits the disposition decision.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with GP follow-up within 48 hours - the 0/1 hour algorithm rules out NSTEMI in this patient given the trivial 1-hour delta
options.rationale: She is in the observe zone (not low rule-out and not rule-in), so discharge without further workup is unsafe; a third troponin and provocative testing or CTCA are required (ESC 0/1h algorithm; Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).

options.text: Diagnose NSTEMI now and proceed to inpatient invasive coronary angiography within 24 hours with adjunctive dual antiplatelet loading and fondaparinux
options.rationale: Diagnosis of NSTEMI on the 0/1 hour algorithm requires absolute delta above the rule-in cut-off (greater than 6 ng/L for hs-cTnI in this assay) or a value above the rule-in threshold; her delta is 4 ng/L and absolute value 18 ng/L is below the rule-in (ESC; Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Repeat troponin at 3 hours from presentation and admit to a cardiac observation unit pending the result and further risk stratification
options.rationale: Patients in the observe zone require a third hs-cTnI at 3 hours; if values plateau without rule-in delta, intermediate-risk pathways (CTCA or exercise/stress testing) apply, with admission for observation until a diagnosis is made (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS; ESC 0/1h algorithm).

options.text: Commence fibrinolysis with weight-adjusted tenecteplase given high cardiovascular risk profile and the early presentation to a non-PCI capable facility today
options.rationale: Fibrinolysis is contraindicated in NSTE-ACS - it increases bleeding without improving outcomes; reperfusion in NSTE-ACS is invasive (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer for outpatient exercise stress echocardiogram in 2 weeks and discharge home tonight with safety-netting and short-term GTN spray as required
options.rationale: Deferring testing for 2 weeks while in the observe zone risks missed evolving infarction; she requires inpatient repeat troponin and provocative or anatomical testing before discharge (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).

stem: A 76-year-old woman with newly diagnosed persistent non-valvular atrial fibrillation attends a cardiology clinic. She has type 2 diabetes on metformin, hypertension on amlodipine, and a transient ischaemic attack 3 years ago with full recovery. She has CKD stage 3b with creatinine 138 micromol/L, eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73 m2, and weighs 58 kg. Her haemoglobin is 122 g/L, platelets 230 x 10^9/L, and she has no history of GI bleeding or peptic ulcer. She is community-dwelling, manages her own medications, and has no plans for elective surgery. She takes no interacting medications (no rifampicin, no azoles, no anticonvulsants). She prefers fewer monitoring visits and is reliable with twice-daily dosing. The clinic registrar specifically asks whether apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily or the standard 5 mg twice daily is correct given the weight and creatinine, whether dabigatran 150 mg twice daily would be safer with her renal function, whether warfarin titrated to INR 2.0-3.0 should be preferred, and whether dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel would be a reasonable alternative for stroke prevention in this patient. She has no DOAC contraindication.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate anticoagulation?

options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: CHA2DS2-VA = 6 (age greater than or equal to 75 = 2, diabetes = 1, hypertension = 1, prior TIA = 2). Apixaban dose-reduction criteria require any 2 of: age greater than or equal to 80, weight less than or equal to 60 kg, creatinine greater than or equal to 133 micromol/L. She meets weight and creatinine criteria, so 2.5 mg BD is correct (CSANZ AF Guideline; AMH).

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: She meets two apixaban dose-reduction criteria (weight 58 kg and creatinine 138 micromol/L), mandating the 2.5 mg BD dose; the 5 mg dose would lead to over-anticoagulation and increased bleeding risk (AMH; CSANZ AF).

options.text: Dabigatran 150 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: Dabigatran is contraindicated in CrCl less than 30 mL/min; with eGFR 32 her CrCl is borderline and dabigatran is not preferred when other DOACs (apixaban) have a wider renal safety margin (AMH; CSANZ AF).

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 indefinitely
options.rationale: Warfarin is acceptable but not preferred for non-valvular AF in the absence of mechanical valve, moderate-severe mitral stenosis, or antiphospholipid syndrome; DOACs have lower intracranial bleeding rates and do not require INR monitoring (CSANZ AF).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily plus clopidogrel 75 mg daily
options.rationale: Dual antiplatelet therapy is inferior to anticoagulation for stroke prevention in AF (ACTIVE-W) and is not recommended for stroke prophylaxis (CSANZ AF).

stem: A 72-year-old man presents with exertional dyspnoea, one episode of syncope while climbing stairs last week, and a 2-month history of chest tightness on hills. He has hypertension on amlodipine, hyperlipidaemia on atorvastatin, and is otherwise well. On auscultation there is an ejection systolic murmur best heard at the right second intercostal space, radiating to both carotids, with a soft S2 and a slow-rising carotid pulse. The murmur is intensity grade 3/6, peaks late in systole, and is louder on squatting. There is no S3, no opening snap, no MR jet, and no pulmonary congestion clinically. His ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy with strain pattern. CXR shows a borderline-enlarged cardiac silhouette without pulmonary oedema. He has never had an echocardiogram. The team specifically asks about each candidate discriminator - the murmur location at the right upper sternal edge, ECG LVH alone, exertional syncope, the slow-rising carotid pulse with narrow pulse pressure, and the dynamic response of the murmur to squatting - and which one best separates severe AS from HOCM today at the bedside. The team specifically wants the one most reliable bedside test.
lead_in: Which finding most strongly distinguishes severe aortic stenosis from hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy in this patient?

options.text: Murmur intensity increases on squatting and falls with Valsalva strain
options.rationale: In AS, increased venous return on squatting augments LV stroke volume and thus murmur intensity, whereas HOCM murmur diminishes on squatting (more LV filling reduces outflow tract obstruction). This dynamic response is the most reliable bedside discriminator (Heart Foundation/CSANZ Valvular Heart Disease; eTG Cardiovascular).

options.text: Presence of an ejection systolic murmur at the right upper sternal edge today
options.rationale: Both AS and HOCM produce systolic murmurs in the precordial/parasternal area; location alone does not discriminate (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: ECG showing left ventricular hypertrophy with associated strain pattern in lateral leads
options.rationale: LVH occurs in both severe AS and HCM; ECG cannot reliably differentiate them (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Exertional syncope as a presenting symptom in this elderly patient with murmur
options.rationale: Exertional syncope occurs in both severe AS and HOCM and is a high-risk feature in both (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

options.text: Presence of a slow-rising carotid pulse and a narrow pulse pressure today
options.rationale: Pulsus parvus et tardus does favour AS, but the question asks the strongest discriminator and is asking for a dynamic manoeuvre; squatting response is more specific and is reproducible at the bedside in the same exam (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

stem: A 78-year-old woman presents with 2 days of progressively worsening dyspnoea and pleuritic right-sided chest pain. She had a right total knee replacement 4 weeks ago with delayed mobilisation. She has hypertension on amlodipine, osteoarthritis, and no prior VTE. She is on no anticoagulation since stopping enoxaparin 1 week post-op. She has had no haemoptysis but her right calf is mildly swollen with 2 cm difference compared with the left. On arrival she is haemodynamically stable, SpO2 92 percent RA improving to 96 percent on 2 L. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm with no S1Q3T3 and no right heart strain pattern. CXR is unremarkable. D-dimer is 720 microg/L (laboratory standard cut-off 500 microg/L; age-adjusted cut-off age x 10 microg/L for age greater than 50). The ED registrar specifically asks whether the age-adjusted D-dimer threshold of 780 microg/L safely excludes PE in this patient, whether CTPA with empirical anticoagulation is appropriate, whether V/Q scan should be first-line given the normal CXR, whether to repeat D-dimer in 24 hours, and whether bilateral lower-limb compression ultrasound should precede any chest imaging today.
lead_in: Based on Wells score and D-dimer interpretation, which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home - D-dimer is below the age-adjusted threshold of 780 microg/L so PE is excluded
options.rationale: Age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10) is only applied in patients with LOW pretest probability (Wells less than or equal to 4, or two-tier 'PE unlikely'). Her Wells score is 7.5 (clinical DVT signs 3, PE likely as most probable diagnosis 3, HR greater than 100 1.5, recent surgery 1.5), so the D-dimer is not applicable and CTPA is required (eTG; Thrombosis & Haemostasis Society of Australia; BTS).

options.text: Order CT pulmonary angiogram and commence empirical therapeutic anticoagulation while awaiting imaging
options.rationale: Her Wells score is high (greater than 4, 'PE likely'); D-dimer should not be used as a rule-out and CTPA is the first-line imaging. Empirical anticoagulation (LMWH or DOAC) is recommended while awaiting imaging if it is delayed beyond a few hours and bleeding risk is acceptable (eTG; THANZ; BTS).

options.text: Order V/Q scan as first-line imaging given the normal CXR
options.rationale: V/Q is appropriate when CTPA is contraindicated (severe contrast allergy, severe renal impairment, pregnancy where it may be preferred), but CTPA is first-line when available (eTG; THANZ).

options.text: Repeat D-dimer in 24 hours and arrange outpatient CTPA if it rises
options.rationale: Delaying imaging in a high-probability patient is unsafe; PE mortality is concentrated in the first 24 hours (eTG; THANZ).

options.text: Arrange bilateral lower-limb compression ultrasound first and only proceed to CTPA if positive
options.rationale: Lower-limb US is useful when imaging the chest is contraindicated, or to add evidence of VTE in equivocal cases, but it is not the first-line test when PE is the principal clinical concern (eTG; THANZ).

stem: A 64-year-old man presents to ED with 3 days of fever, productive cough with rusty sputum, right-sided pleuritic chest pain, and progressive dyspnoea. He has a 25 pack-year smoking history, hypertension on perindopril, and no known drug allergies. He has not travelled recently and has had no contact with unwell people. He has not had a pneumococcal or influenza vaccine in the past 12 months. On arrival he is alert but unwell-looking. His CXR shows dense right middle lobe consolidation with a small parapneumonic effusion. Bloods show WCC 18.4 x 10^9/L with neutrophilia, CRP 280 mg/L, albumin 28 g/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L, urea 9.2 mmol/L, glucose 7.8 mmol/L. ABG on room air shows pH 7.42, pO2 58 mmHg, pCO2 36 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L. The ED registrar specifically asks whether to admit to a general ward with oral amoxicillin and doxycycline, to discharge home with oral moxifloxacin based on a CURB-65 of 1, to admit to a general ward on IV benzylpenicillin monotherapy, or to admit to ICU with empirical IV vancomycin and meropenem covering MRSA and Pseudomonas. HDU and ICU beds are available.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate disposition and initial empirical antibiotic therapy?

options.text: Admit to general ward today with oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for atypical cover
options.rationale: His SMART-COP score is 6 (SBP less than 90 = 2, multilobar/effusion 1, RR greater than or equal to 25 1, tachycardia greater than or equal to 125 0, confusion 0, oxygenation pO2 less than 70 = 2, pH normal 0, albumin less than 35 = 1; SBP scores 2). With SMART-COP greater than or equal to 5 (high risk of needing intensive respiratory or vasopressor support), oral therapy and general ward are inadequate (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Admit to high-dependency or ICU; IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus IV azithromycin 500 mg daily, with empirical cover for atypicals
options.rationale: SMART-COP greater than or equal to 5 indicates a high risk of needing intensive respiratory or vasopressor support and warrants HDU/ICU-level care. Empirical therapy for severe CAP in Australia is IV ceftriaxone plus IV azithromycin (Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory).

options.text: Discharge home with a 7-day course of oral moxifloxacin 400 mg once daily based on a CURB-65 of only 1 today
options.rationale: Even if CURB-65 underestimates severity in this case, his hypotension, hypoxia, and metabolic derangements meet inpatient criteria; SMART-COP is more sensitive for need for intensive support in younger patients (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Admit to general ward today with intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g six-hourly monotherapy and review at 48 hours for response to therapy
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin monotherapy does not cover Mycoplasma, Legionella, or atypicals and is inadequate empirical therapy for severe CAP requiring ICU-level support (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Admit to ICU today with intravenous vancomycin 1.5 g twelve-hourly plus intravenous meropenem 1 g eight-hourly for empirical broad-spectrum coverage
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum antipseudomonal and MRSA cover is reserved for patients with risk factors (structural lung disease, recent hospitalisation, recent broad-spectrum antibiotics, MRSA colonisation); he has none (eTG Respiratory).

stem: A 76-year-old woman presents at 3 am with 8 hours of progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and a dry cough. She has HFrEF (LVEF 35 percent) on perindopril, bisoprolol, frusemide, and dapagliflozin; type 2 diabetes on metformin; and known paroxysmal AF on apixaban 5 mg twice daily. She was discharged 10 days ago after a 5-day admission for cellulitis. Since then she has been less mobile, sitting in her chair most of the day. She denies fever, chest pain, or haemoptysis. On arrival she is afebrile but distressed. JVP is elevated to the angle of the jaw at 60 degrees. There are fine bibasal crackles to the mid-zones and trace ankle oedema bilaterally. Her ECG shows AF at 118 bpm without acute ischaemic changes. CXR shows bilateral interstitial opacities with upper lobe blood diversion. Troponin I is 22 ng/L (cut-off less than 16, stable on repeat). NT-proBNP is 6800 ng/L. D-dimer is 1800 microg/L. Bedside lung ultrasound shows bilateral B-lines and small bilateral pleural effusions. Right calf circumference is 1 cm greater than left without erythema. Creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 110). The ED registrar specifically asks whether to manage as ADHF with IV diuretic, nitrate and reserve CTPA for inadequate response, whether to treat as suspected PE with immediate CTPA and empirical LMWH despite therapeutic apixaban, whether to treat as severe community-acquired pneumonia with IV ceftriaxone and azithromycin, whether to activate the cath lab for primary PCI given the troponin elevation, or whether to defer imaging and shotgun all three pathways simultaneously.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial diagnostic and management step?

options.text: Treat as acute decompensated heart failure with IV frusemide and GTN, and arrange CT pulmonary angiogram only if response is inadequate within 6 hours
options.rationale: Clinical, biochemical, and POCUS evidence (elevated JVP, NT-proBNP greater than 4000, B-lines, upper lobe diversion, congestion) points to ADHF as the primary problem; the elevated D-dimer is non-specific in HF and AF on apixaban makes PE unlikely. Initial management is decongestion with IV diuretic and nitrate, with CTPA reserved for inadequate response or evolving features of PE (Heart Foundation/CSANZ HF; eTG; THANZ).

options.text: Treat as suspected pulmonary embolism with immediate CT pulmonary angiogram and empirical therapeutic-dose enoxaparin, withholding all diuretics until the imaging result is available for review
options.rationale: She is on therapeutic apixaban, has no haemodynamic instability, and her presentation is dominated by congestive features rather than pleuritic chest pain or focal hypoxia. D-dimer is elevated as expected in HF/inflammation and is not a rule-in test (THANZ; eTG).

options.text: Treat as severe community-acquired pneumonia with intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus intravenous azithromycin 500 mg daily and admit to a high-dependency monitored bed for observation
options.rationale: She is afebrile with no productive cough or focal consolidation; CXR pattern and BNP favour congestion not infection. Antibiotics are not first-line in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (eTG Respiratory).

options.text: Activate the cardiac catheter lab for emergency primary percutaneous coronary intervention given the troponin elevation, AF with rapid ventricular response and bibasal pulmonary congestion on examination
options.rationale: The minor troponin elevation is stable and likely demand-related from the AF with rapid ventricular response and decompensated HF; there is no ECG evidence of STEMI and no NSTEMI dynamic rise. Primary PCI is not indicated (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).

options.text: Defer all imaging and start broad empirical combination therapy targeting pulmonary embolism, community-acquired pneumonia and acute decompensated heart failure simultaneously without further bedside diagnostic disambiguation today
options.rationale: Shotgun therapy without diagnostic clarity exposes her to harm (broad antibiotics in non-infection, bleeding from triple-pathway anticoagulation on apixaban). The clinical picture is dominated by congestion and warrants targeted decongestion first (Heart Foundation/CSANZ HF; eTG).
